findings.description: First page layout is not preserved; the memorandum content is pushed far down the page and separated from the header block by excessive whitespace and an inserted horizontal rule.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the logo/header and memorandum title/body appear together in the upper half of page 1 with normal spacing. In the HTML, there is a very large blank area after the top header/footer region, then the memorandum title starts much lower on the page with a full-width rule above the content.

findings.description: Reading flow on page 1 is disrupted by duplicated header elements and delayed start of the main content.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents header branding followed directly by the memorandum. In the HTML, the governor/secretary/director names appear again near the right side before the memorandum begins, and the main text starts much later, altering the visual reading sequence.

findings.description: Page 2 layout is broken by splitting the content into separate top and bottom blocks with a large empty gap between them.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, sections I and II continue continuously down the page. In the HTML, the top of page 2 begins with leftover outline items ('III. Written Public Comments Summary', 'IV. Attachments'), followed by a large blank area and then the Section I heading starts much lower, indicating misplaced grouping and broken page structure.

findings.description: Page 2 reading order does not match the PDF due to misplaced outline items appearing before Section I.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 2 starts with 'I. History/Background'. The HTML page 2 begins with 'III. Written Public Comments Summary' and 'IV. Attachments' before later showing 'I. History/Background', which changes the sequence a reader encounters.

findings.description: Section heading styling is weakened on page 2.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, section headings such as 'I. History/Background' and 'II. March 24, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary' are bold and visually prominent. In the HTML, headings are larger but less distinctly emphasized, with lighter weight and reduced contrast from body text.

findings.description: Page 3 content is fragmented by a large blank gap in the middle of the page.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the oral comments bullets continue seamlessly and then section III begins on the same page. In the HTML, a footer and colored bar appear mid-flow, followed by a large empty area before the bullet list resumes at the bottom, disrupting structure.

findings.description: Page 3 reading order is interrupted by content being split around a large empty region.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents a continuous list of oral comments. In the HTML, the 'Environmental' bullet is cut off, then after a large gap the continuation begins with 'alleging that wildlife and pets...' at the bottom, forcing the reader to jump visually.

findings.description: Page 4 layout is similarly broken with section III split across widely separated regions.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, section III starts mid-page and its bullet list continues normally. In the HTML, the section III heading and opening paragraph appear near the top, then after another large blank area the bullets resume near the bottom after a colored bar.

findings.description: Page 4 reading flow is disrupted by splitting a single bullet item across distant parts of the page.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the 'Environmental' bullet in section III is continuous. In the HTML, the bullet begins near the top and continues only after a large gap lower on the page, making the sequence harder to follow.

findings.description: Page 5 attachment section is pushed to the bottom with excessive unused space, unlike the source layout.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, 'IV. Attachments' appears near the top of the page with the full list beneath it. In the HTML, preceding bullets occupy the upper area, then a very large blank region appears, and 'IV. Attachments' does not begin until near the bottom.

findings.description: Page 5 visual flow is altered by continuation text dominating the page before the attachments heading appears.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 5 immediately presents the attachments section. In the HTML, the remainder of section III is shown first and the attachments heading appears much later, so the page-level sequence differs.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy does not fully reflect the document structure because the top-right official names block was not represented as headings and all major sections were flattened to h2 under a single h1. This is a structural mismatch, though the section titles themselves are accurate.
findings.evidence: PDF shows centered document title "MEMORANDUM" followed by numbered sections "I. History/Background," "II. March 24, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary," "III. Written Public Comment Summary," and "IV. Attachments." HTML uses one h1 for "MEMORANDUM" and h2 for each numbered section. The governor/secretary/director block present in the PDF header is rendered as plain paragraphs instead of structural headings.

findings.description: HTML introduces nested/repeated page structure markers and duplicated footer blocks within content flow, causing content repetition that was only page-level decoration in the PDF.
findings.evidence:
PDF contains one footer per page and page images/logos as page artifacts. HTML repeats footer text multiple times inside the main reading flow, e.g., "State of North Carolina | Environmental Quality | Energy, Mineral and Land Resources ... 919 707 9200" appears after the opening section, again inside the Environmental bullet under oral comments, again inside the Environmental bullet under written comments, and again near the end. HTML also contains nested
elements and multiple separators corresponding to page images, which creates repeated content blocks not part of the logical text.
